name: Mint Pizza Dough and Shrimp Topping
recipeIngredient: 1/2 pound Shrimp (optional, can use more to your liking; omit mozzarella if desired)
Mint or Cilantro or Basil - to taste
recipeInstructions:
NOTE: Original recipe used 1 tablespoon... we reduced it.
NOTE: Can use more shrimp to your liking. We used three-quarters of a pound and omitted the mozzarella as Jeff doesn't tolerate lactose in cheese. Shell, devein, cook, and dice the shrimp. The original recipe did not use garlic for topping; I added it and found it delicious with the shrimp.
NOTE: 1-2 cups (optional), we left the mozzarella out completely and really tasted the wonderful flavor of the shrimp and feta.
If using chopped fresh mint, use 3 tablespoons or a scant 1/4 cup. This dough is superb topped with any Greek or Middle Eastern topping.
Bread Machine Method:
Warm liquids to lukewarm (110°F). Add ingredients in the order specified for your machine. Use the dough cycle, which will knead the dough and allow it to rise on time. It is not necessary to let the dough rise longer than one hour. If your machine has a double kneading dough cycle, remove the dough after an hour or so and turn off the machine. Allowing the dough to knead a second time causes bubbles that are difficult to roll.
Food Processor Method:
Warm liquids to lukewarm (110°F). Combine all dry ingredients (including yeast) in a food processor bowl (steel blade) for 10 seconds. Either active dry or rapid rise yeast can be used; mix it with the flour. Pour oil and remaining liquid ingredients through the feeding tube until the dough forms a ball. If the dough is sticky, add flour one tablespoon at a time until the dough is not sticky. Process into a ball, place in a greased bowl, cover with a kitchen towel, and let sit in a warm, draft-free location for 50-60 minutes.
Heavy-duty Mixer with Dough Hook Method:
Prepare dough as you would for a food processor. Allow to knead for about five minutes. Add just enough flour to cause the dough to cling to the dough hook. Let rise according to food processor instructions.
By Hand Method:
If using quick or rapid rise yeast, mix it with the flour; no proofing necessary. If using active dry yeast, place 1/4 - 1/3 of the warm water in a large bowl and sprinkle a pinch of sugar and yeast into the water. Stir until dissolved and let sit in a warm, draft-free location for five minutes. Add remaining water (liquid) and oil and stir well. Add all remaining ingredients and stir together until no longer workable with a spoon. Turn out on a lightly floured counter. Pat dough into a ball and flatten slightly. Fold the dough over and use heels of hands to push dough away from you with rolling motion, rotating one quarter each time. Continue kneading for 5-10 minutes. Place dough in a large greased bowl, cover, and let rise in a warm draft-free location (such as an oven or microwave) for 50-60 minutes.
Preheat oven to 475°F for at least 30 minutes if using a pan; from 30 minutes to 1 hour if using a stone.
Grease the pizza pan with olive oil or spray it with nonstick vegetable spray. If using a peel and stone, dust the peel with cornmeal to prevent the dough from sticking so you can easily slide it onto the stone. Or cut a parchment paper circle slightly larger than your pizza, place it on the peel, and make your pizza on the paper. Slide the pizza and paper onto the stone.
After the dough has risen once, divide into halves and roll out on a lightly floured or cornmeal-covered countertop or hand stretch the dough into a circle. Roll or stretch from the center outwards until a circle is formed. Dust with flour or cornmeal as needed to prevent sticking; thinner dough results in a thinner, crispier crust. Lay dough on prepared pan or peel, pulling up around the edges of the pan to form a slight lip and crimping the edges.
For a thin, crisp crust, top immediately and bake on the bottom rack of the oven directly on the pizza stone until golden brown (5-10 minutes). For a thicker, chewier crust, allow to rise for 15-30 minutes before topping and baking. The longer the dough rises, the thicker it becomes.
Makes one (15") or two (10") pizzas
Entered into MasterCook and tested by Reggie & Jeff Dwork ()
